coredata.dc:title: Development of novel blue emissive carbon dots for sensitive detection of dual metal ions and their potential applications in bioimaging and chelation therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Chattopadhyay, Sruti
Mehrotra, Neha
Jain, Swati
Singh, Harpal
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, there has been an increased interest in the use of fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) as an alternative to toxic synthetic organic dyes in live cell imaging, medical diagnostics and other diverse biomedical applications. In the present work, highly fluorescent C-dots have been prepared from commercially available peptic digest of animal tissue, without using any additional chemical reagent for surface functionalization. The synthesized C-dots are monodispersed nanomaterials with hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino surface functionalities and remain stable in high ionic strength media and over a wide range of pH. They exhibit a strong blue fluorescence upon excitation at 350 nm with a high quantum yield of 61%. The potential use of these C-dots as fluorescent nanoprobe for specific metal ions (Cu2+ and Hg2+) detection through chelation, radical scavenging and cellular imaging has been investigated. These highly fluorescent labels show rapid cellular internalization without hampering cellular integrity. Cell proliferation assay demonstrates that these C-dots have minimal cell toxicity even at a high concentration of 3 mg/mL. They show significant quenching on incubation with cells pretreated with Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions. They also show rapid exocytosis from cells showcasing their high potential for chelation therapy. Thus, this study offers a scalable synthesis approach for highly fluorescent and bio-compatible C-dots from a cost-effective precursor, which can be used for specific metal ions detection and their removal from cells through chelation via exocytosis. They can also be used as an alternate to synthetic organic dyes for fluorescence-based cell-imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Metal ions
Quenching
Chelation
Fluorescence
Bioimaging
Cytotoxicity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing of metal ions by fluorescence quenching
Study of radical scavenging activity of C-dots
Cellular model for metal ion chelation
Detection of Cu2+ and Hg2+ using C-dots as sensing probe
Study of radical scavenging activity of C-dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM images of C-dots deposited on a copper grid coated with an ultrathin Holey carbon film. Inset: Line and arrows indicate the lattice fringes with lattice spacing of 0.234 nm and particle size distribution histogram of C-dots (b) AFM image, inset: corresponding height profile of C-dots (c) DLS measurement in aqueous solution showing particle size distribution of C-dots (size 3.43 nm and PDI 0.238) and (d) Zeta potential of C-dots in aqueous solution (–23.6 mV), (e) ATR-FTIR spectrum, (f) EDX, (g) XRD pattern and (h) Raman spectrum showing D and G bands of C-dots.

Study of optical properties of C-dots (a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum, (b) the maximum excitation and emission spectra, (c) fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths from 310 to 420 nm and (d) fluorescence intensity at various pH from 3 to 12

(i) Effect of different metal ions on FL intensity of C-dots), Selectivity study - Interference of common metal ions on FL intensity of (ii) C-dots:Cu2+, (iii) C-dots:Hg2+, Interference of common anions on FL intensity of (iv) C-dots:Cu2+ and (v) C-dots:Hg2+ (λex = 350 nm; [Mn+] = 60 µM; [C-dots] = 5 µg/mL), (vi) Fluorescence life time decay curve of C-dots (inset) and in presence of Cu2+ or Hg2+ (Ex at 350 nm, Em at 431 nm)
Effect of conc. of (a) Cu2+ and (b) Hg2+ on FL quenching of C-dots (λex = 350 nm)

Cellular imaging of MDA MB-231 cells using synthesized C-dots (A) Time dependent study: a) 30 min, b) 1 h, c) 2 h and d) 3 h (Concentration of C-dots 100 µg/mL), (B) Concentration dependent study: a) 25 µg/mL, b) 50 µg/mL, c) 100 µg/mL, d) 200 µg/mL and e) 400 µg/mL (Incubation time 2 h), (C) C-dots as a sensing probe for Cu2+ and Hg2+ in live cells (a) Cells + C-dots, (b) cells + Cdots:Cu2+ (c) cells + Cdots:Hg2+ and (D) Exocytosis of C-dots in MDA MB-231 cells (a) 0 h, (b)1h, (c) 3 h, (d) 6 h, (e) 16 h and (f) 24 h (Concentration of C-dots 100 µg/mL and incubation time is 2 h). The scale bar corresponds to 50 µm.
Schematic presentation of synthesis of C-dots using hydrothermal method
Schematic representation of interaction of meal ions with C-dots for fluorescence quenching
Comparison of various reported fluorescent sensors for detection of Cu2+ and Hg2+.

head.ce:title: Development of novel blue emissive carbon dots for sensitive detection of dual metal ions and their potential applications in bioimaging and chelation therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Biomedical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Delhi, New Delhi 110016, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Biomedical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology-Delhi, N.D.-16, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: All Indian Institute of Medical Sciences, New Delhi 110029, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: All Indian Institute of Medical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: All Indian Institute of Medical Sciences, New Delhi 110029, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Amity Institute of Nanotechnology, Amity University, Sec. 125, Noida, Uttar Pradesh, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Amity Institute of Nanotechnology, Amity University, Sec. 125, Noida, Uttar Pradesh, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Science and Technology, Technology Bhavan, New Mehrauli Road, New Delhi 110016, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Science and Technology, Technology Bhavan, New Mehrauli Road N.D.-16, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Centre for Biomedical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Delhi, New Delhi 110016, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, there has been an increased interest in the use of fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) as an alternative to toxic synthetic organic dyes in live cell imaging, medical diagnostics and other diverse biomedical applications. In the present work, highly fluorescent C-dots have been prepared from commercially available peptic digest of animal tissue, without using any additional chemical reagent for surface functionalization. The synthesized C-dots are monodispersed nanomaterials with hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino surface functionalities and remain stable in high ionic strength media and over a wide range of pH. They exhibit a strong blue fluorescence upon excitation at 350 nm with a high quantum yield of 61%. The potential use of these C-dots as fluorescent nanoprobe for specific metal ions (Cu2+ and Hg2+) detection through chelation, radical scavenging and cellular imaging has been investigated. These highly fluorescent labels show rapid cellular internalization without hampering cellular integrity. Cell proliferation assay demonstrates that these C-dots have minimal cell toxicity even at a high concentration of 3 mg/mL. They show significant quenching on incubation with cells pretreated with Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions. They also show rapid exocytosis from cells showcasing their high potential for chelation therapy. Thus, this study offers a scalable synthesis approach for highly fluorescent and bio-compatible C-dots from a cost-effective precursor, which can be used for specific metal ions detection and their removal from cells through chelation via exocytosis. They can also be used as an alternate to synthetic organic dyes for fluorescence-based cell-imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last few decades, the rapid development in the field of nanotechnology has led to the discovery of numerous nanoparticles with a multitude of applications in diverse fields spanning from physical to life sciences. Amorphous carbon, graphite, and diamond were the only three known natural carbon allotropes till the end of the 1970s. However, the last three decades saw the discovery and thorough characterization of various carbon nanomaterials like carbon nanohorns, nanodots, nanotubes, nano-onions, fullerenes, graphene derivatives, etc. [1]. In particular, carbon nanodots (C-dots) as a new member of this nano-family has drawn enormous attention as an optical sensor due to their distinctive properties like, ultra-small size with excellent fluorescent property, high water dispersibility, good biocompatibility, excellent photo and chemical stability. Moreover, due to their low toxicity and strong photoluminicence (PL) property, they are also used as an alternative candidate to synthetic organic dyes and semi-conducting metal-based quantum dots (QDs) for various purposes. Nowadays C-dots are used as a fluorescence probe in various applications such as drug [2] and gene delivery [3], in-vitro [4] & in-vivo [5] bioimaging, and selective as well as sensitive detection of metal ions [6], pathogens [7] and organic molecules [8–11].

The use of C-dots for the sensitive and selective detection of environmentally and biologically relevant metal ions especially in aqueous media, is of great interest [12]. Copper is an important micronutrient and is essential in many biological reactions. The deficiency of Cu2+ hampers enzyme activity, inhibits cell metabolism whereas excess Cu2+ in body damages liver as well as kidney and causes gastrointestinal disturbance,promotes production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and causes neurodegenerative disorders [13]. Mercury ions (Hg2+) are one of most toxic heavy metal ions and possess serious threat to environment and health even if present in very small quantity. Hg2+ can easily absorb through skin, gastrointestinal tissues and cause permanent damage to human liver and central nervous system [14,15]. It is, therefore, extremely important to detect Cu2+ and Hg2+ in living organisms. In addition, there are several reports on fluorescence-based metal ion detection due to its high sensitivity, fast analysis and easy accessibility. A detailed comparative study on various metal ions detection including different types of C-dots, sensitivity, detection limit and linear range was reported by Gao et al [16].

The physico-optical properties of the C-dots can be altered by adopting different synthesis strategies and various precursors. They can be designed through surface functionalization or passivation by introducing functional groups like hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl which can produce surface defects. Depending on the size and type of functional groups present on the surface of C-dots, their emission wavelength varies, [17] rendering them with diversified characteristics. Their synthesis strategies can be broadly divided into two categories, bottom-up and top-down methods. The former includes fabrication of C-dots from molecular precursors by high pressure and temperature [18], thermal pyrolysis etc. while the latter refers to the cleavage of larger carbon source into nanosized particles [19]. There are different approaches for the synthesis of C-dots such as electrochemical etching, laser ablation, arc discharge, hydrothermal, solvothermal, acidic, chemical, ultrasonic exfoliations and microwave irradiation [19–23]. Among these, hydrothermal method is one of the most efficient and hassle-free routes to synthesize size controlled, stable C-dots through liquid phase chemical reactions with minimum loss of materials and minimal purification steps preserving their yield. Various sources of carbon have been reported in literature for synthesis of C-dots spanning natural precursors [24–30] to organic molecules [31] and macromolecules [32]. Use of natural precursors is a popular choice due to their easy availability and straightforward synthesis protocol [33]. But these C-dots suffer from serious drawbacks: extremely low material yield and luminescence intensity i.e. quantum yield (3–15%) compared to the well-established heavy metal (cadmium, selenium etc.) based quantum dots. To overcome this problem, the surface of C-dots has been passivated with electronegative heteroatoms such as nitrogen [7], sulphur [34], phosphorous [35]. As a result, fluorescence intensity of C-dots tremendously increased owing to charge transfer between the electron-rich heteroatoms and electron deficient carbon moiety. Among these, nitrogen-based compounds are mostly employed as a dopant or surface passivating agent to increase the fluorescence intensity.

In the current study, highly fluorescent, blue emissive C-dots are synthesized in large-scale from peptic digest of animal tissue (beef extract powder) via hydrothermal process for the first time to the best of our knowledge. The advantage of choosing this nitrogen containing precursor is to passivate the C-dot surface without using additional chemical reagents such as ethylene diamine [7], urea [11], amino acids [23] and Tris [36]. Moreover, during hydrothermal process, functional groups are introduced onto the surface of C-dots due to the decomposition of nitrogen containing compounds already present in the precursor which lead to highly fluorescent C-dots comparable to synthetic organic dyes. In addition, the carbon source and single-pot synthesis protocol are both cost-effective and eco-friendly, facilitating diverse applications. The present paper deals with evaluation of developed C-dots as a fluorescent nanoprobe for quantitative detection of Cu2+ and Hg2+ metal ions and their potential applications in bioimaging and chelation therapy due to low cytotoxicity and rapid uptake in mammalian cells.

Different lots of beef extract powder were purchased from HiMedia Laboratories (RM002) of two different batch numbers (0000360904 and 0000421938). 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-Diphenyltetrazolium Bromide (MTT) assay kit and Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) were purchased from HiMedia Laboratories Pvt. Ltd. Various metal salts, concentrated hydrochloric acid and methanol were procured from Fisher Scientific. Sodium hydroxide pellets, quinine sulphate and 2, 2΄-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) were purchased from Merck Specialties, Central Drug House and Sisco Research Laboratories respectively while Fetal bovine serum and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were bought from Sigma Aldrich. MDA MB-231 human breast cancer cell line was procured from NCCS Pune, India. Milli-Q water (18 Ω) from the Direct Q Millipore system was used for the entire length of the experiment.

Surface functionalized, highly fluorescent C-dots were synthesized via hydrothermal method. 1.2 g of beef extract powder (from different lots) was dissolved in 10 mL of Milli-Q water and transferred into a 25 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept in oven to maintain constant temperature for 5 h. Different temperatures 90°, 120°, 150°, 180 and 210 °C were used for synthesis optimization to obtain highly efficient fluorescent C-dots with maximum yield and fluorescent property. After 5 h, the autoclave was allowed to cool down undisturbed and the light brown colored solution obtained was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant containing fluorescent C-dots was filtered through 0.22 µm filter and finally purified by dialysis against deionized water using a dialysis membrane (100 Da MWCO) for 24 h and stored in a dark, cool place till further use. The C-dots dispersion was characterized by UV–visible absorption and fluorescence emission spectra.

Fluorescence quantum yield of C-dots was calculated via a relative method using quinine sulphate solution in 0.1 M sulphuric acid as reference. The integrated fluorescence intensity and the absorbance values (less than 0.05) of the synthesized material and quinine sulphate were measured and the fluorescence quantum yield was calculated using the following formula:
∅
x
=
∅
st
I x
A
st
η
x
2
I
st
A x
η
st
2
where, ∅x is the fluorescence quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, A is the absorbancy value and η is the refractive index. x and st refer to test and standard fluorescence substance of known quantum yield (0.546) respectively. The synthesized C-dots solution was freeze-dried for 36 h and the dried quantity of the C-dots was calculated gravimetrically.

UV–Visible absorption spectra of the prepared C-dots were measured on UV/visible/NIR spectrometer packaged with Lambda 25 software (Perkin Elmer Inc. USA) using quartz cuvettes of 1 cm path length. Fluorescence spectra measurements were recorded using Perkin Elmer LS 55 Fluorescence Spectrometer with an excitation and emission slit width of 7.5 nm and 5 nm respectively subjected to 650 Gain. The Raman scattering spectra were acquired through InVia Raman Spectrometer (Renishaw, UK). The typical accumulation time used in these experiments was 10 sec with an integration time of 1 sec. 50X long working distance objective lens was used for focusing the samples which were excited with 785 nm NIR laser light source (300 mW) with 1% laser power. Dispersion of scattered light was done using a 1200 lines/mm grating. Morphological assessment was carried out on High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) (TECNAI, FEI, USA) at an acceleration voltage of 200 KV. 10 µL of dilute solutions of C-dots was placed on 200 mesh copper grid coated with Holey carbon films and air dried to obtain a thin coating of the sample and was directly fed in the instrument at 27 °C. The surface topology of C-dots was studied by Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) (Innova®, Bruker) by scanning sample in tapping mode. Dilute solution of C-dots was drop casted on silicon wafer as substrate and air dried. Attenuated Total Reflection Fourier Transform Infra-Red Spectrum (ATR FT-IR) using Perkin Elmer Spectrum One FT-IR Spectrometer was recorded of the solid moisture freed C-dots powder sample. Elemental composition was analyzed by Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) (JEOL, JSM-7800F) by mounting solid sample and particle size by Anton Paar Litesizer 500 Particle Analyzer using dilute solution of C-dots. All the measurements were recorded at room temperature. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was done by using a Rigaku Ultima IV diffractometer equipped with Ni-filtered Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å). The diffraction patterns of the sample were recorded in the 2θ range 20–70° at a scanning rate of 4°/min. Time Resolved Fluorescence (TRFL) spectra of C-dots solutions in presence and absence of metal ions were recorded on time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC), Edinburgh Instruments FLSP 920 using a pulsed laser diode (377 nm) with a pulse period of 500 kHz. The instrument response function was measured with a Ludox scattering solution in distilled water. The decay curves were best fitted in Origin Pro 8.5 software using a triple exponential decay function.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of metal ions by fluorescence quenching
ce:section.ce:para:
2 mL of C-dots solutions (5 µg/mL) were taken in separate eppendorf tubes and different concentrations of various metal ion solutions (in µL) were added and incubated for 3 min. 1 mL of each solution was transferred to cuvette and fluorescence spectrum as well as intensity were measured at 350 nm excitation wavelength. All the measurements were done at neutral pH and at room temperature. For highly sensed metal ions (Cu2+ and Hg2+), quenching efficiency was determined by using Stern-Volmer equation-
(2)
F 0
/ F =
K q
[ C ]
+ 1
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of C-dots in the absence and presence of metal ions. Kq is the slope of the linear fit and [C] is the concentration of metal ion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of radical scavenging activity of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para:
The radical scavenging activity of C-dots was tested by DPPH assay. Different concentrations of C-dots solution ranging from 5 to 350 μg/mL, was added to 1 mL of 0.1 mM DPPH solution prepared in methanol. The reaction mixtures were thoroughly vortexed and kept in the dark at room temperature for 30 min and then absorbance was measured spectrophotometrically at 517 nm.
(3)
%
Radical scavenging activity of C
-
dots =
Ao - A s
Ao
× 100
where A0 and As represents absorbance of DPPH without and with C-dots.

Cell viability was evaluated using 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. Cytotoxicity of synthesized C-dots was assayed using MDA MB-231 human breast cancer cells maintained in DMEM media supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum. MDA MB-231 cells were seeded at a cell density of 5X103 cells per well in 96 well plate and incubated in a humidified incubator at 37 °C, 5% CO2 for 24 h. The cells were then incubated with C-dots for 24 h at varying concentrations (125–3000 µg/mL). 10 µL of freshly prepared MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added to each well and incubated for 2 h. Post incubation, the culture medium was discarded and the formazan crystals were solubilized using 100 µL DMSO. The absorbance of the plate was measured by using a microtiter plate reader (EPOCH, Biotek, USA) at 570 nm with reference wavelength of 670 nm. The percentage viability was determined as the ratio of absorbance of treated cells to absorbance of the untreated control cells.

Cellular uptake of C-dots in MDA MB-231 cells was studied in time and concentration dependent manner. For both the experiments, 2X105 cells were seeded on glass cover slips in 12 well plates and allowed to adhere for 24 h before exposure to C-dots. For the time dependent cellular uptake, the cells were exposed to 100 µg/mL C-dots for varying time periods (0.5–3 h) and then rinsed thrice with phosphate buffered saline (1X PBS, pH 7.4). The glass cover slips were inverted on microscope slides and the cells were imaged by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) with 40X objective and 405 nm excitation laser (Fluoview FV-1000, Olympus, Japan). For the concentration dependent uptake studies, the cells were exposed to varying concentrations of C-dots (25–400 µg/mL) for a common incubation time of 2 h. Post incubation, the cells were washed and imaged as mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular model for metal ion chelation
ce:section.ce:para: 2X105 MDA MB-231 cells were seeded on glass cover slips in 12 well plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. The cells were then exposed to 10 µM Cu2+ and Hg2+ salt solutions for 1 h. Following incubation, the cells were washed twice with PBS (pH 7.4) to remove excess metal salts and fresh media with C-dots (100 µg/mL) was added and incubated for 2 h. After washing the cells were imaged as mentioned in Section 2.7.

Exocytosis of internalized C-dots was studied as a function of time. 2X105 MDA MB-231 cells were seeded on glass cover slips in 12 well plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. The cells were then exposed to 100 µg/mL C-dots for 2 h. Following the 2 h incubation, the cells were washed thrice with PBS (pH 7.4) and fresh media without C-dots was added to the wells. The cells were visualized using CSLM at different time points (0, 1, 3, 6, 16 and 24 h) to study the exocytosis of C-dots.

In this study, highly fluorescent C-dots were prepared from peptic digest of animal tissue via hydrothermal method (Scheme1 ). The color of the sample changed to pale yellow after heating at different temperatures ranging from 90° to 180 °C indicating the formation of C-dots as shown in Fig. S1a. The optimal carbonization temperature was chosen at 180 °C as it provided maximum material yield and fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 61% (Fig. S1b) which is much higher as compared to the C-dots (QY 3–20%) obtained from other natural sources reported in the literature [19]. The high QY indicated that the surfaces of C-dots are well passivated [37,38]. The dried quantity of prepared C-dots was calculated to be 125 mg/mL with material yield of 63%. As can be seen in Fig. S1a, the synthesized C-dots had excellent dispersibility in aqueous medium, transparent pale yellow colour in day light with emission of bright blue fluorescence under UV light (365 nm). The as prepared C-dots were also highly dispersible in other polar solvents like DMSO, PBS and ethanol which can be useful for future chemical modifications for drug loading and other potential biological applications. In order to check the reproducibility of these C-dots in terms of material yield and QY, different batches of beef extract powder were used to synthesize C-dots and purified according to the protocol mentioned in the Section 2.2. It was observed that the QY and the material yield of these C-dots are comparable even though different batches of beef powder were used for synthesis. Total nitrogen content in these precursors is in the range of 12–12.5 %, as a result the percentage of dopant (nitrogen) and passivation on C-dots surface is almost same in synthesized nanomaterial irrespective of the different batches.

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were analyzed to investigate the morphology and particle size of C-dots as shown in Fig. 1 a. It shows the size distribution analysis of the C-dots as observed via HRTEM (inset). The particle size was calculated using Image J software with n = 100 particles and mean diameter was found to be 3.11 nm. The size distribution was found to be in a range of 1–6 nm with the majority C-dots being 3 nm in size. HRTEM image (inset) also indicates their crystalline nature while the lattice spacing for C-dots was 0.234 nm which is the characteristics of graphitic carbon (101 facet) [29].

The atomic force microscope (AFM) analysis (Fig. 1b) on a silicon substrate also confirmed their spherical shape and well dispersion in aqueous media. The height profiles (inset) revealed that the typical topographic heights were in the range of 0.5–3 nm and confirmed the size distribution. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement in aqueous solution confirmed narrow particle size distribution of C-dots with an average size of 3.43 nm (Fig. 1c). Zeta potential of –23.6 mV (Fig. 1d) attributed to the high stability and presence of a large number of negative charges on the surface of C-dots.

In order to investigate the functional components and structural aspect of C-dots, ATR-FTIR, EDX, XRD and Raman spectroscopy were carried out. ATR-FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1e) analysis gave information about surface functionalities of C-dots. The peak at 3349 cm−1 indicated the O–H stretching vibration of surface hydroxyl groups, whereas the peaks at 2962 cm−1, 2873 cm−1 were attributed to aliphatic C–H stretching and the 770 cm−1 peak corresponded to the out-of-plane bending vibrations of aromatic C–H bond. The band at 1642 cm−1 and the weak one at 1300 cm−1 corresponded to C = O and C-O stretching vibrations of carboxylic ester groups respectively. The peaks at 1580 cm−1, 1455 cm−1, 1404 cm−1 and 1109 cm−1 confirmed the aromatic C = C stretching vibration, C-N bond, COO– stretching and asymmetric stretching vibrations of C-NH-C respectively. Hence, ATR-FTIR studies proved that the synthesized C-dots possess hydroxyl, carboxyl and amide functional groups on their surface enabling significant passivation and easy dispersion in polar solvents, requisite for biological applications. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (Fig. 1f) was used to obtain the elemental composition of C-dots. The characteristics EDX peaks indicated the major components were Carbon (52.9%) and Oxygen (43.3%) with a small amount of Nitrogen (3.7%) supporting the ATR-FTIR results.

XRD pattern (Fig. 1g) of lyophilized C-dots revealed five Bragg's diffraction peaks at 2θ ~ 27.3°, 31.74°, 45.44°, 56.44° and 66.26° which are reflections from the 010, 002, 101, 004 and 110 planes respectively [29]. This confirmed the presence of both graphitic as well as turbostratic carbon in the C-dots. The intense peaks at 31.74° ( 002) and 56.44° ( 004) indexed to the hexagonal graphitic crystal structure and corresponded to their inter-chain distance of closely packed alkyl chains [39]. The diffraction peaks at 45.44° ( 101) and 66.26° ( 110) characterized the 2D in-plane symmetry along the graphene layers. The Raman spectrum of C-dots (Fig. 1h) yielded two bands, G (crystalline) and D (disorder) at 1650 and 1434 cm−1, respectively. The G band indicates the presence of sp2 carbon atoms in a 2D hexagonal lattice whereas D band corresponds to the vibrations of disordered carbon with dangling bonds in the termination plane and is usually an indication of the defects in C-dots [40,41]. The intensity ratio of D and G bands (ID⁄IG ) was found to be 0.33 which represents the disorder extent and the ratio of sp3/sp2carbons, implying that C-dots were more crystalline in nature than amorphous [42,43]. This observation was also supported by XRD analysis. The detailed chemical analysis indicated the presence of surface passivating groups facilitating aqueous dispersibility beneficial in biosensing and bioimaging applications.

To explore the optical properties of the synthesized C-dots, UV–Vis absorbance and fluorescence spectra were recorded as shown in Fig. 2 . The absorption spectrum (Fig. 2a) showed two peaks at 245 and 360 nm corresponding to π-π* and n-π* transitions of C = C and C = O bonds respectively which were responsible for the photo luminescence (PL). C-dots showed an intense PL emission peak centered at 431 nm, upon excitation at 350 nm wavelength (excitation maxima) (Fig. 2b). To further investigate the photoluminescence properties, they were excited with different wavelengths varying from 310 to 420 nm (Fig. 2c). It was observed that when the excitation wavelength increased from 310 to 350 nm, the emission peak became stronger with slight red shift whereas upon excitation from 350 to 420 nm, the emission peak shifted to 471 from 431 nm with sharp decrease in PL intensities suggesting that the emission spectra were sensitive to the excitation wavelength. This is an intrinsic property seen in most of the C-dots reported in literature [44]. These optical properties of C-dots are generally due to differently sized particles, presence of various diffraction planes (for crystalline C-dots), surface defects, different distributions of emissive trap sites on individual C-dots etc. [29,45]. Moreover, it is also reported that luminescence properties depend on the size and organic functional groups present in the carbon source, which controls the distribution of excited state energy, charge separation, and holding of electrons and holes in the C-dots and their radiative recombination [43]. In order to evaluate the stability of the synthesized C-dots in various conditions, the related experiments were also performed. The time dependent stability study of C-dots was carried out to find change in PL intensity and QY over a long period of six months as shown in Fig. S2. No significant change in PL intensity of C-dots (concentration 5 µg/mL at 350 nm excitation) and QY was observed during this period. These results confirmed that the synthesized C-dots are efficient in terms of fluorescence and possess long storage stability. The effect of ionic strength on the stability of C-dots was also studied by preparing C-dots solution (5 µg/mL) in 10 mM PBS (pH = 7.4) with various concentrations of NaCl and KCl in the range of 0 to 1 M. The PL intensities of C-dots remain almost unchanged when excited at 350 nm (Fig. S3). The effect of pH was also measured using 5 µg/mL aqueous dispersion of C-dots at the respective pH ranging from 3 to 12. As revealed in Fig. 2d, the PL intensities first increased as the pH value increased from 3 to 4 and then exhibited minimal variation in the range of 4–8 with gradual decrease in solutions with pH greater than 8 when excited at 350 nm. This trend indicates the presence of surface functionalities which are influenced by changing pH. The surface of C-dots was mostly covered with hydroxyl and carboxyl functionalities which are sensitive at lower and higher pH as also supported by ATR-FTIR results [10]. The above results demonstrated that the synthesized C-dots are highly stable with good tolerance for ionic strength and pH and thus can be used as a promising candidate for potential applications in diagnosis and therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ and Hg2+ using C-dots as sensing probe
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, the selectivity tests of C-dots towards Cu2+ and Hg2+ were performed by adding 30 µM of various common interfering cations and anions into solutions of C-dots:Cu2+ or C-dots:Hg2+ and the results (Fig. 3(ii) to (v) confirmed that the presence of interfering ions have negligible impact on Cu2+ or Hg2+ detection. Now we explored the feasibility of using these C-dots for the detection of Cu2+ and Hg2+ in the concentration range of 0–20 µM. Fig. 4 a and 4b show a gradual decrease in PL intensity at 431 nm with increasing Cu2+ and Hg2+ concentrations respectively which was due to fluorescence quenching. Sensitivity of detection of metal ions- Cu2+ and Hg2+, was evaluated by Stern-Volmer equation. As shown in Fig. 4 the plot of relative fluorescence response of C-dots (F0/F) vs metal ion concentrations [C] is highly linear with correlation coefficients (R2) = 0.982 and 0.970 for Cu2+ and Hg2+ respectively. Limit of detection (LOD) was estimated using 3δ/S method, where δ stands for the standard deviation of blank signal (n = 5) and S is the slope of linear plot. LOD of Cu2+ and Hg2+ were calculated to be 86 and 79 nM respectively at neutral pH thus showcasing an excellent potential of the synthesized C-dots as fluorescent probes for Cu2+ and Hg2+ detection. In addition, the LOD of Cu2+ is much lower than the maximum permissible concentration recommended by USA Environmental Protection Agency (20 µM) and the World Health Organization (WHO) (30 µM) in drinking water. Table1 represents the comparison of various fluorescent sensors for the detection of Cu2+ and Hg2+ recently published in the literature which shows that our results are better than most of the published data [12,47–53].

Further, the practical real sample analysis was carried out for the analytical application of the synthesized C-dots for using as a fluorescence probe for the detection of Cu2+ and Hg2+ in tap water and canal water samples by standard spiking method. The results are given in Table 2 . The recoveries were in the range of 92–106% suggesting the analytical applicability and reliability of detection of these two metal ions by the developed C-dots probe.

Though Cu is a biologically important metal and an essential micronutrient but in excess, it can have adverse impact on body whereas mercury (Hg) is a highly toxic metal and if present in any extent, causes severe damage. Chelation therapy is one of the preferred medical treatments to reduce the metal toxicity. Chelating agents can form complexes with toxic metal ions present in intra and extracellular spaces thus facilitating their easy excretion from the body e.g. calcium disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (CaNa2EDTA) [54] is one of the commonly used chelating agents for reducing metal toxicity (Pb). The current study demonstrates that C-dots can effectively interact with Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions and hence we can explore their suitability and potential use in chelation therapy for Cu and Hg toxicity. Therefore, we evaluated their scavenging activity against DPPH and biological activity - cytotoxicity, cellular uptake and exocytosis in human cell line.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of radical scavenging activity of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: There are many pathophysiological processes which are related with the accumulation of free radicals. Antioxidants prevent and stabilize the damage caused by free radicals by supplying electrons to these damage cells. Cu2+ is well known for promoting the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) via Haber Weiss reaction [55]. ROS react with DNA, membrane lipid, protein causing an oxidative stress which is responsible for cellular damage. Thus, the assessment of the radical scavenging activity of C-dots can be useful to avoid the oxidative damages induced by Cu2+ and Hg2+. There are several reports on the antioxidant property of C-dots [56,57]. The hydrogen atom donating ability of the C-dots was determined by the decolorization of methanol solution of 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) which is one of the commonly used methods to evaluate radical scavenging activity. This molecule develops violet color in methanol and fades to yellow color in the presence of C-dots upon formation of DPPH-H. It is clearly seen from Fig. S7 that percentage radical scavenging activity linearly increased with concentration of C-dots till 200 µg/mL and then became saturated at 275 µg/mL concentration due to the formation of stable DPPH-H complex upon combining with a hydrogen radical. Amount of C-dots required for 50% scavenging activity (EC50) is calculated from the graph as 129.2 µg/mL confirming effective radical scavenging properties. Literature survey of radical scavenging activity of C-dots synthesized from various sources showed EC50 values in a range of 80–375 µg/ml [56,58]. C-dots derived from Catharanthus roseus [46] showed EC50 of 132 µg/mL whereas diamine functionalized graphene quantum dots exhibits 30% scavenging activity (EC30) at concentration of 342 µg/mL using 2,2́-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulphonic acid) radical (ABTS.+) [57]. Therefore, the C-dots synthesized in the present study exhibit similar or better radical scavenging activity as compared to other reported C-dots.

Cytotoxicity studies of the synthesized C-dots were performed on MDA MB-231 human breast cancer cell line with C-dot concentrations ranging from 125 to 3000 µg/mL. A clear dose dependent effect on the cell viability was observed, however the viability remained more than 80% even on exposure to the highest concentration 3000 µg/mL (Fig. S8) thus showcasing their potential for use in various biological applications.

The synthesized C-dots showed efficient time dependent uptake in MDA MB-231 cells. As shown in Fig. 5 A, visible fluorescence could be observed in the cells as early as 30 min after exposure, indicating easy transport across the cellular membrane. Increased incubation time with the C-dots leads to increased fluorescence intensity, with a slight signal saturation observed in the cells when incubated for 3 h. Optimal fluorescence signal was observed in cells incubated for 2 h and therefore this incubation time was fixed for further cellular uptake studies.

The concentration dependent cellular uptake studies were carried out using five different C-dots concentrations of 25, 50, 100, 200 and 400 µg/mL. Even the lowest concentration of 25 µg/mL produced a clearly detectable level of fluorescence within the cells after an incubation of 2 h and the fluorescence signal intensity increased in a concentration-based manner with the highest concentration resulting over saturation (Fig. 5B). For an incubation of 2 h, the optimal C-dots concentration was identified as 100 µg/mL. The higher concentrations can be used in experiments that require a smaller incubation time. As mentioned earlier, on exposure to C-dots, the cells maintained a viability of more than 80% even at 3000 µg/mL, therefore a concentration range of 100–400 µg/mL for cellular imaging is well within the safety window of C-dots cytotoxicity and leaves room for concentration increase as per application requirement. The low cytotoxicity of the synthesized C-dots along with efficient cellular uptake and high fluorescence yield makes them a promising candidate for future bioimaging applications. We have also studied the chelation of C-dots with metal ions within live cells. When incubated with only C-dots, cells showed strong emission whereas in presence of Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions (M2+ = 10 µM), the FL intensity reduced significantly (Fig. 5C). This result, along with their stability in high ionic strength media (Fig. S3), suggests that C-dots can be used as a fluorescence probe for monitoring Cu2+ and Hg2+ in live cells even at very low concentration by FL quenching. Exocytosis of C-dots was also studied for varying time periods (0–24 h). It was clearly observed from the CLSM images (Fig. 5D) that C-dots started to disappear from the cells within 1 h and the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with time. Negligible fluorescence was observed at 24 h which implied that majority of C-dots had been effluxed by the cell transport system or degraded by the cellular lysosomes. The fast exocytosis demonstrated by C-dots is advantageous for their use as chelating agents as it would facilitate rapid removal of Cu2+ or Hg2+ ions from the cells post complex formation.

A simple and efficient method is developed for large scale synthesis of C-dots from a cost-effective and common carbon source with high material and quantum yield (61 %) via hydrothermal process without using any additional chemical reagent for surface functionalization. Synthesized C-dots have average diameter of 3.11 nm and emit strong blue fluorescence in the UV range. They are crystalline in nature with surface functionality of hydroxyl, carboxyl and amine groups, and exhibited excellent monodispersity, dispersion stability in various polar solvents, long term storage and photostability. Their use as a highly selective and sensitive fluorescent probe for the detection of Cu2+ and Hg2+ cations has been demonstrated and LOD of 83 and 79 nM was calculated for these cations. The synthesized C-dots exhibited good biocompatibility in MTT assay and showed efficient cellular uptake when incubated with MDA MB-231 human breast cancer cell line. Even at low concentration of 25 µg/mL, C-dots penetrate cell membrane within 30 min giving quantifiable fluorescence signal. Significant fluorescence quenching of C-dots was observed on interaction with metal ions in cellular model, which along with complete removal of C-dots from cells within 24 h can be useful for therapy in case of metal ion toxicity. High photoluminescence property, selective complexation with the metal ions, efficient cellular uptake, rapid exocytosis and good biocompatibility confirm their potential in detection, bioimaging and chelation therapy.

Sruti Chattopadhyay: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Validation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Neha Mehrotra: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Swati Jain: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Harpal Singh: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors acknowledge financial support given by Department of Health Research (DHR), Govt. of India (12013/15/2019-HR).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106706.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
